basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Layl — “The Night” — unfolds in twenty-one verses what al-Sharḥ and al-Ḍuḥā said in eight or eleven: the great law of the two ways. The sūra opens with four oaths — the night, the day, the male and the female, their creation — to reach a truth one would wish lighter: inna saʿyakum la-shattā, “your efforts are scattered apart.” Qushayrī, on this verse, draws up a striking inventory of human efforts: one seeks his world, another the appetites of his soul and obedience to his passion (hawā, هَوى), another seeks honour and ambition; but also, higher, the one who refines his godfearing (taqwā, تَقْوى), the one who purifies his remembrance (dhikr, ذِكْر), the one who stands in beautiful contentment (riḍā, رِضَا), and the one who seeks his Master (mawlā, مَوْلى). Then comes the verdict: to the one who gives, who fears God and confirms the sovereign Good (al-ḥusnā, الحُسْنى), God will smooth the easy way; to the one who withholds, thinks himself self-sufficient and denies that Good, He will smooth the hard way. The sūra closes on Abū Bakr's freeing of the slave Bilāl — not from any calculation, but seeking only the Face of his Lord, the Most High. It is the very portrait of the ṣiddīq (صِدِّيق), the one whose effort, among all those that scatter apart, goes straight.

versets.trad: By the night, when it veils!
versets.commentaire: yaghshā (يَغْشَى): to veil, to cover, to envelop. The descending night covers the horizon and all that lies between sky and earth; its darkness wipes out the contours. Qushayrī already opens the inward reading: “The night, for those in perplexity (taḥayyur, تَحَيُّر), covers every space of their thought; they no longer find the way.” There is an outer night and an inner night: the one in which the soul, no longer able to discern, waits for a light it cannot itself produce.

mots.def: ghashiya: to envelop, to cover, to veil; imperfect.

versets.trad: By the day, when it shines forth!
versets.commentaire: tajallā (تَجَلَّى) — a word central to Sufi thought. The verb names luminous manifestation, unveiling, epiphany. It is the word the Quran uses for God's manifestation on Mount Sinai (7:143). Qushayrī, mirroring verse 1, reads: “The day of the people of gnosis (ahl al-ʿirfān, أَهْل العِرْفَان) is the radiance of their hearts and of their innermost secrets, to the point that nothing remains hidden for them; they are at rest with the rising of the sun — the sun of unification (tawḥīd, تَوْحِيد) — and need no longer kindle the lamp” of reason. The true day is inward: the hour when the heart, illumined, no longer needs to search.

mots.def: nahār: day, as opposed to night.
tajallā: to manifest, to unveil, to shine forth; key word in Sufi thought for divine manifestations.

versets.trad: By that which He created — the male and the female!
versets.commentaire: The pronoun mā (مَا) can mean either “that which” (by the creative power itself) or “He who” (by the One who created). Qushayrī reads the second: it is God Himself by whom the oath is sworn, through His act of creating. Male and female, in the Quran, are the archetypal image of every polarity (cf. 51:49: “Of every thing We created a pair”). That the oaths thus cover night and day, then male and female, is to say in images: everything is dual, and the servant too stands within the balance of these dualities.

mots.def: introduces the object of the oath: He who (or: that which His power) created.

versets.trad: Truly, your efforts are scattered apart:
versets.commentaire: Key verse. saʿy (سَعْي): effort, walk, striving — the very movement by which a life unfolds. shattā (شَتَّى): scattered, divergent, each going its own way. Qushayrī then unrolls a striking inventory of human efforts, as though letting faces file past: “One is in the pursuit of his world; another in the appetites of his soul and obedience to his passion; another in the pursuit of his honour and ambition; another in concern for the hereafter; another in the perfecting of his godfearing; another in the purifying of his remembrance; another in the station of contentment; another — in the pursuit of his Master.” And at the summit: “The one who joins the effort of the soul in obedience, the effort of the heart in sincerity (ikhlāṣ, إِخْلَاص), the effort of the body in nearness, the effort of the tongue in remembrance of God and gracious speech with people, calling creatures to God and offering them sincere counsel.” The sūra does not describe two ways only, but a multitude — and each must know which is his.

mots.def: saʿy: effort, tense walk; verbal noun of saʿā. With plural suffix pronoun.
shattā: plural of shatīt; scattered, divergent, each going his own way.

versets.trad: as for the one who gives in charity and fears God,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds the verse in several superposed senses. The plain reading: aʿṭā — he gave of his wealth; ittaqā — he guarded himself from going against his Lord. Then a chivalric reading, which touches the heart of futuwwa (فُتُوَّة, the virtue of spiritual chivalry): “aʿṭā: he gave to others fairness coming from himself; ittaqā: he guarded himself from demanding fairness for himself.” To give others what one could have demanded for oneself: such is the higher generosity. And deeper still: “aʿṭā: he turned away from the two abodes (this world and the next as objects of desire); ittaqā: he guarded himself from giving them any rank in his heart” — so that the heart, empty of all, might be pure for God alone.

mots.def: aʿṭā: to give, to give in charity; perfect.
ittaqā: to guard oneself, to fear God reverently; same root as taqwā.

versets.trad: and holds the sovereign Good as true,
versets.commentaire: al-ḥusnā (الْحُسْنى) — “the most beautiful” — is a Quranic word covering several graces: the best reward, Paradise, the promise of pardon for great sinners, the Prophet's intercession, God's “compensation” for one who knows how to lose for Him. Qushayrī lists some of these senses, but what matters is the verb: ṣaddaqa, “to hold as true, to confirm.” The sovereign Good is not only an object of belief — it is what must be dared to hold as true even before one sees it. Faith is the organ of this daring trust.

mots.def: ṣaddaqa: intensive form of ṣadaqa; to hold true, to confirm. Same root as ṣidq (truthfulness) and ṣiddīq.

mots.sens: the sovereign Good, the most beautiful
mots.def: ḥusnā: the most beautiful (feminine); abstract feminine superlative of ḥasan. In the Quran: supreme reward, Paradise, pardon, sovereign Good.

versets.trad: We shall ease his way to happiness.
versets.commentaire: nuyassiruhu li-l-yusrā (نُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى): We shall make him easy to the easy way. Qushayrī unfolds what this concretely means: “We shall make acts of obedience easy for him, We shall make transgressions distasteful to him, We shall give him appetite for nearness, We shall endear faith to him, We shall make benevolence (iḥsān, إِحْسَان) beautiful in his heart.” And he adds: “The keeping in His obedience, and the return to what was begun in worship.” God's grace, to the one set on the right way, is not first to flatten the ground — it is to make what is right desirable. The servant's happiness is to take taste for what God loves.

mots.sens: We shall make easy for him
mots.def: yassara: to make easy, to facilitate; intensive form of yasara. With suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

mots.def: yusrā: the easy way, ease; feminine superlative of yusr.

versets.trad: But as for the miser who deems himself rich,
versets.commentaire: The verse turns the mirror. bakhila (بَخِلَ): he was miserly, he held back what he should have given. istaghnā (اسْتَغْنَى): he believed himself rich in himself, he dispensed with God. It is the very word of sūra 96 (raʾāhu staghnā) — the illusion of self-sufficiency as the root of all rebellion. Qushayrī draws them together: “Bakhila: he refused what was due. istaghnā: he held himself without need in his belief.” Two acts that look opposed — to withhold what one has, and to think oneself without need — but in reality the same gesture: that of a closing heart.

mots.def: bakhila: to be miserly, to hold back what should be given; perfect.
istaghnā: to dispense with, to deem oneself without need; perfect. Root gh-n-y: wealth, sufficiency.

versets.trad: and denies the existence of the sovereign Good,
versets.commentaire: kadhdhaba (كَذَّبَ) — the exact opposite of ṣaddaqa in verse 6. Where the one held true, the other holds as a lie. The symmetry is exact: to each of the three traits of the just answers its inverse. Giving / withholding; godfearing / pride of self-sufficiency; faith / denial. And the consequence of the next verse follows the same symmetry.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: intensive form; to hold as a lie.

versets.trad: We shall ease his way to hardship.
versets.commentaire: A verse that should make one tremble. God does not say He will impose the hard way on the one who denies Him; He says He will make it easy for him. Qushayrī: “He will fall into transgression without having premeditated it, and We shall lay in his hands the occasions of disobedience.” The idea is terrifying in the gentleness of its logic: from withholding and denying, the soul ends up finding natural what it first fled. One does not close in one go; one closes little by little, until it becomes easy to no longer love.

mots.sens: We shall make easy for him
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

mots.def: ʿusrā: the hard way, hardship; feminine superlative of ʿusr.

versets.ar: وَمَا يُغْنِي عَنْهُ مَالُهُ إِذَا تَرَدَّىٰ
versets.tr: wa-mā yughnī ʿanhu māluhu idhā taraddā
versets.trad: His wealth shall avail him nothing when he falls.
versets.commentaire: taraddā (تَرَدَّى): he collapses, he falls headlong, he perishes. Qushayrī simply: “when he dies, what will his wealth avail him after his death?” The verse is a question of evidence — and precisely because it is evident, it ought to be enough to open the eyes. But evidence, as the Quran says elsewhere, does not suffice so long as the heart has grown used to not seeing.

mots.def: aghnā: to enrich, to avail, to suffice; imperfect.
māl: wealth, property; with suffix pronoun.
taraddā: to fall headlong, to perish; perfect.

versets.trad: Truly, upon Us rests the Guidance,
versets.commentaire: Major verse. God places upon Himself an obligation: ʿalaynā, “upon Us.” As if He were committing Himself. Qushayrī specifies: “Upon Us rests the guidance for Our friends (awliyāʾ, أَوْلِيَاء), those We have oriented.” And more broadly: “through the proofs” God sows in creation and in the heart. The servant does not have to invent the way: it will be shown to him. He has only not to refuse to see it.

mots.def: preposition ʿalā + 1st pers. plur. suffix pronoun.
hudā: the straight direction, guidance; verbal noun of hadā.

versets.trad: and to Us belong the next life and the present!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “As kingdom, which We give to whom We will.” The two worlds — this world and the next — belong to the same King. There can therefore be, for the servant, no strategy that would gain from one by sacrificing the other: all is under the same hand. That alone is enough to dissolve the illusion of being able to “calculate”.

mots.def: preposition li- + 1st pers. plur. suffix pronoun.

versets.trad: I have warned you, then, of a blazing Fire,
versets.commentaire: talaẓẓā (تَلَظَّى): blazes, burns with its full ardour. The warning is not meant to terrify — it is meant to awaken. The sūra moreover does not close on the Fire, but on the contentment of the just (v. 21). The Fire is the consequence, not the end.

mots.def: andhara: to warn, to caution; perfect 1st pers. sing. + plural suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. sing.

mots.def: talaẓẓā: to blaze, to burn ardently; imperfect (contracted form).

versets.trad: in which none shall burn but the wretched,
versets.commentaire: al-ashqā (الأَشْقى): the most wretched, the one who has entered shaqāwa (شَقَاوَة), the state of the soul that has undone itself. The word is not only a judgment on another: it is the image of what a life becomes when it persists in refusal. The next verse will name the two acts by which one enters this state.

mots.def: ṣaliya: to burn, to be exposed to fire; imperfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ashqā: superlative of shaqī; the most wretched, who has entered shaqāwa.

versets.trad: the one who cries lies and turns away.
versets.commentaire: Two verbs, two gestures: kadhdhaba (كَذَّبَ), to deny, and tawallā (تَوَلَّى), to turn away. Qushayrī, briefly: “that is, he disbelieved.” Denial is rarely merely intellectual; it is almost always accompanied by a movement of the body — turning the back, leaving. Sūra 96 named these two verbs as mirror; they will be found nearly everywhere in the Quran, forming together the typical attitude of denial.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: to hold as a lie.
tawallā: to turn away, to turn one's back.

versets.trad: But the most godfearing shall be kept far from it,
versets.commentaire: al-atqā (الأَتْقى): the most pious, the one who has made taqwā (تَقْوى) the center of his life. Taqwā is not servile fear; it is the guarding of the heart, the delicacy toward what is owed to God. And one will be kept far from the Fire — not as an outer reward, but as an inner consequence: the soul that has guarded itself finds itself, when the moment comes, guarded in turn.

mots.sens: he will be kept far from it
mots.def: jannaba: to keep far from, to set aside; passive imperfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: passive imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: atqā: superlative of taqī; the most pious. Root of taqwā.

versets.trad: the one who gives of his wealth to purify himself.
versets.commentaire: yatazakkā (يَتَزَكَّى): he purifies himself, he sanctifies himself. The root z-k-w is splendid: it means both “to purify” and “to make grow.” Giving purifies the giver — not by magic, but by undoing the invisible chains the held wealth ties around the heart. Qushayrī adds two complementary readings: “he gives the obligatory zakāt”; “he purifies himself from sins.” In Islam, giving is not one act among others: it is the way by which the soul lightens itself.

mots.def: tazakkā: to purify oneself, to sanctify. Root z-k-w: purify and make grow. Same root as zakāt.

versets.ar: وَمَا لِأَحَدٍ عِندَهُ مِن نِّعْمَةٍ تُجْزَىٰ
versets.tr: wa-mā li-aḥadin ʿindahu min niʿmatin tujzā
versets.trad: No one with Him is to be rewarded for a favour received,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports the occasion of revelation: Abū Bakr, seeing the slave Bilāl tortured by his master because he kept proclaiming Aḥad, Aḥad (“One, One”), went and bought Bilāl from his master, and freed him. The pagans then said: “If Abū Bakr freed Bilāl, it is really to repay a debt he owed him.” And God sent down these two verses to deny the calculation. No one, the verse says, owed him a favour to be repaid. He gave nothing to recover anything; he gave, simply, because.

mots.def: niʿma: favour, grace; here, debt of gratitude.
jazā: to reward; here passive imperfect feminine.
mots.gram: passive imperfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: but only by seeking the Face of his Lord, the Most High:
versets.commentaire: A pivotal verse. ibtighāʾ wajh rabbihi (ابْتِغَاء وَجْهِ رَبِّه), “the seeking of the Face of his Lord”: this is the Quranic expression of pure giving, the giving that aims at nothing other than God. Al-wajh (الوَجْه), the Face, names God Himself insofar as He lets Himself be met — the aspect by which He turns toward the creature and the creature toward Him. Qushayrī: “in order to draw near Him by that giving.” Pure giving is not the erasure of motivation; it is the unification of motivation. A single end: Him. And all the rest falls silently in line. An old Sufi word says: “Sincerity (ikhlāṣ) is that your outer acts and your inner states are equal — when no one sees you, and when everyone sees you.”

mots.def: ibtighāʾ: verbal noun of ibtaghā; to seek, to desire.
wajh: face, countenance; in Quranic theology, names God insofar as He lets Himself be met.
aʿlā: superlative of ʿālī; the highest, the Most High. Divine Name.

versets.trad: then, at last, he shall obtain contentment.
versets.commentaire: Final verse, that closes the sūra. yarḍā (يَرْضَى): he will be content. Qushayrī, in few words, opens the horizon: “God will be content with him, and he will be content with what God gives him.” Contentment is mutual — as in the great formula that recurs elsewhere in the Quran (5:119; 98:8): raḍiya Llāhu ʿanhum wa-raḍū ʿanhu, “God is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him.” The sūra, opened with the inventory of all the diverging efforts, closes thus on the only joy that counts: the reciprocal contentment between servant and Lord. It is the point of all spiritual life; and the freed Bilāl as much as the freeing Abū Bakr meet within it.

mots.def: raḍiya: to be content, to accept; imperfect.
